findings.title: Default page limit is 1000 on skills/departments/services list endpoints — 10× above recommended ceiling
findings.code_evidence:
async findAll(page = 1, limit = 1000, category?: SkillCategory) {
    const safeLimit = Math.min(limit || 1000, 1000);
findings.description: The skills `findAll` (and identically departments.service.ts:80-81, services.service.ts:102-103) defaults to limit=1000 and clamps at 1000. The PERFORMANCE checklist ceiling is 100. Every call that omits `limit` fetches all rows including nested `_count` relations. The same pattern is repeated verbatim in DepartmentsService.findAll and ServicesService.findAll.
findings.root_cause: Developer chose a 1000-row default/ceiling to avoid needing pagination logic on the frontend, treating the API as a thin wrapper over the full table rather than a paginated resource.
findings.impact: On a moderately large org (hundreds of skills/departments/services), every list page load fetches the full table. Response payloads can exceed 100 KB, increasing serialization time and network egress. Callers can also force `limit=1000` explicitly, making the ceiling ineffective as a resource guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Lower default to 50 and hard cap to 100: `const safeLimit = Math.min(limit || 50, 100);`. Apply to all three services (skills, departments, services). Update frontend callers that rely on the all-rows behavior to either paginate or use a dedicated `findAll` (no-pagination) endpoint with RBAC guard.
acceptance_criteria: GET /skills returns at most 100 rows per page when limit is absent or > 100
Same cap applies to GET /departments and GET /services
A query with limit=9999 returns exactly 100 rows, not 9999
findings.notes: Same pattern at apps/api/src/departments/departments.service.ts:80-81 and apps/api/src/services/services.service.ts:102-103 — all three need the same fix.

findings.title: importSkills() issues one INSERT per row in a serial for-loop with no upper bound on input array size — N sequential DB roundtrips
findings.description: The import endpoint accepts an `ImportSkillsDto` whose `skills` array has no `@ArrayMaxSize` validator (confirmed by reading import-skills.dto.ts). For N items, `importSkills` issues N sequential `prisma.skill.create()` calls inside a for-loop. A user with `skills:create` permission (ADMIN/RESPONSABLE) can accidentally or intentionally POST an oversized payload (e.g. a full CSV of thousands of rows) and tie up a DB connection for the entire duration. There is no batch INSERT or transaction wrapper either.
findings.root_cause: No upper-bound decorator on the DTO array and no batch INSERT strategy — the service loops individually over every element.
findings.impact: A 10 000-item import request ties up one connection for ~10+ seconds (one roundtrip per insert). Multiple concurrent such requests from authorized users exhaust Prisma's connection pool, blocking all other API traffic. Even legitimate large imports (hundreds of skills) add unnecessary round-trip latency when `createMany` would be a single query.
findings.suggested_fix: 1. Add `@ArrayMaxSize(500)` (or a business-appropriate ceiling, e.g. 200) to `ImportSkillsDto.skills` in import-skills.dto.ts. 2. Replace the loop with `prisma.skill.createMany({ data: validSkills, skipDuplicates: true })` inside a single transaction. Collect validation errors before the batch rather than per-row.
acceptance_criteria: A valid import of 200 skills completes in a single DB round-trip (verified via query log)
No connection pool starvation when 5 concurrent large-import requests are made by authorized users
findings.notes: validateImport (line 576) has the same loop but only performs one pre-load findMany then Set-based lookups, so no per-row DB call. The missing @ArrayMaxSize still allows oversized payloads to consume memory during JSON parse.

findings.title: findUsersBySkill() fetches ALL users with a skill then filters isActive in JavaScript — inactive users inflate DB result needlessly
findings.description: The `userSkill.findMany` query fetches every user-skill record for a given skill regardless of whether the user is active. The `isActive` filter is then applied in JavaScript (line 515). In an org with many deactivated users this results in: (a) unnecessary data transfer from PostgreSQL, (b) over-fetching the nested `userServices` join for inactive users, (c) `totalUsers` reporting the count after JS-filtering rather than letting the DB compute it efficiently.
findings.root_cause: The `where` clause on `userSkill.findMany` only filters by `skillId` and optionally `level`; the `isActive` guard was added as an afterthought in JavaScript rather than pushed into the Prisma query.
findings.impact: Proportional to the inactive-to-active user ratio. In an organisation that retains deactivated accounts (leavers, contractors), 30–50% of rows fetched per query are discarded. Each row includes a nested `userServices` join, amplifying the wasted data transfer.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `user: { isActive: true }` to the Prisma `where` clause so the filter is pushed to the DB. Then remove the JavaScript `.filter()` on line 515.
acceptance_criteria: EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows a boolean filter on users.is_active at the DB level
API returns only active users for a skill that has both active and inactive assignees

findings.title: findByUser() (GET /personal-todos) issues an unconditional DELETE on every read — unnecessary write roundtrip on each list call
findings.description: Every GET /personal-todos request executes `cleanupOldCompleted`, which unconditionally issues a `deleteMany` with a date filter. Even when there is nothing to clean up this is an extra DB write round-trip on a read path. With a 7-day window, the cleanup result is empty on ~99% of calls (the average user does not complete 20 todos per week and immediately check again). The semantic of 'list my todos' causing side effects also makes the endpoint non-idempotent.
findings.root_cause: Cleanup was embedded in the read path as a convenience so stale todos are always cleared before presenting the list, avoiding a separate cron or scheduled task.
findings.impact: One extra DB round-trip per list call per user. With frequent frontend polling (e.g. dashboard refresh every 30s) this doubles the DB query load from this endpoint. At scale (100 concurrent users) this generates ~200 DB queries/min from a single widget. Severity is limited by the hard 20-item cap on todos — the deleteMany always scans at most 20 rows.
findings.suggested_fix: Move cleanup to a NestJS `@Cron` job. If a synchronous side effect is preferred, at minimum add a cheap count-before-delete check: skip the DELETE if `personalTodo.count({ where: { userId, completed: true, completedAt: { lt: cutoff } } }) === 0`.
acceptance_criteria: GET /personal-todos does not issue a DELETE when no completed todos are older than 7 days
Stale completed todos are still cleaned up (via cron or lazy count-gated delete)
findings.notes: Minor severity given the 20-item hard cap on personal todos — the deleteMany will always scan at most 20 rows. Still a semantic issue and unnecessary write latency.

findings.title: getSkillsMatrix() loads all active users and all skills without pagination — response size grows as O(users × skills)
findings.description: The skills matrix endpoint fetches ALL active users (with nested department + skills includes) and ALL skills in parallel, then cross-joins them in JavaScript. No pagination, no limit. For a large organisation (500 users × 200 skills), the JSON response encodes 500 × 200 = 100 000 cell objects. At ~100 bytes each that is ~10 MB per request. The `users` findMany also eagerly includes `skills { include: { skill: true } }` which is an O(U×S) JOIN from Postgres.
findings.root_cause: The matrix is designed as a reporting view; pagination was not considered because it is used in a grid component that requires the full dataset client-side.
findings.impact: At >200 users the response exceeds the 100 KB payload guideline. At >500 users it can push several megabytes, causing serialization spikes on the API and increasing client memory pressure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add pagination on the users axis (`page`/`limit`) with a hard cap (e.g. 200 users per page). For full-matrix exports, offer a streaming CSV endpoint. At minimum document and enforce an org-size assumption in the response type.
acceptance_criteria: GET /skills/matrix with 600 active users returns at most 200 user rows by default
A `page` parameter allows consumers to paginate through all users
findings.notes: Classified as nit because this endpoint requires `skills:manage_matrix` permission (ADMIN/RESPONSABLE/MANAGER only) and is a reporting tool. Still a genuine payload-size and DB-load issue at scale.

findings.title: recomputeChainFrom() issues one UPDATE per audit row in a serial for-loop — O(N) round-trips hold the advisory lock for the full duration
findings.description: The recompute walk issues one parameterised UPDATE per row inside an async for-loop. For a chain of N rows, this is N sequential DB round-trips within a single long transaction. For a production instance with 50 000 audit rows (login events over ~2 years), this means 50 000 round-trips, taking minutes. Additionally, all rows are loaded into Node.js memory before the walk begins. During this window the `audit_logs_chain` advisory lock is held, blocking all concurrent audit event persistence (every login, leave approval, etc.).
findings.root_cause: Sequential chaining is inherently serial because each row's `prevHash` depends on the previous row's computed `rowHash`. The hashes must be computed in order in JS, but the UPDATEs themselves could be batched.
findings.impact: Maintenance-only script, not a request path. However, during the recompute window the advisory lock blocks all audit event persistence. At 100K rows and 1ms/update, the lock is held for ~100 seconds, causing all concurrent audit events to queue or timeout at the DB connection level.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute all hashes in JS sequentially (unavoidable), collect pairs of `(id, prevHash, rowHash)`, then issue batched `UPDATE … FROM (VALUES …)` SQL statements in chunks of e.g. 1000 rows. This reduces 100K round-trips to ~100. Also stream rows in chunks rather than loading the entire tail at once to bound memory usage.
acceptance_criteria: Full-chain recompute of 100K rows completes in <10 seconds
The advisory lock window is measured and documented in the runbook
findings.notes: This is a maintenance script invoked once after schema migrations. Severity is nit because it does not affect the live request path. However, the advisory lock contention makes it operationally risky on a large production DB.

verified_clean: skills.service.ts: assignSkillToUser — no N+1; checks are by unique key, not loop
skills.service.ts: validateImport — single pre-load findMany then Set-based lookups, no per-row DB call
skills.service.ts: getUserSkills — unbounded but scoped to single user; grouping done in JS over bounded result
departments.service.ts: getDepartmentStats — single findUnique with include, stats computed in JS over bounded result
services.service.ts: getServiceStats — same pattern, bounded result
services.service.ts: getServicesByDepartment — no pagination but department-scoped; organisations have few services per dept
personal-todos/personal-todos.service.ts: create/update/delete — standard single-row operations, no N+1
personal-todos/dto/create-personal-todo.dto.ts — single text field with @MaxLength(200), no array, no fan-out risk
personal-todos/dto/update-personal-todo.dto.ts — same, no array fields
health/health.service.ts: check() — simple SELECT 1 + Redis PING, no fan-out, bounded timeout
deployments/deployments.service.ts: recordBoot — single INSERT + one $queryRaw for migrations; fire-and-forget on boot
deployments/deployments.service.ts: readAppliedMigrations — single raw query, bounded by migration count
audit/audit.service.ts: log() — fire-and-forget persistence, no loop
audit/audit-persistence.service.ts: log() — single transaction with 3 queries (advisory lock + prevRow + insert); no fan-out
audit/export-audit.helper.ts: emitDataExported — single fire-and-forget log call
audit/recompute-chain.ts: in-transaction verification — re-reads the same segment; hash computed in JS, no additional DB round-trips beyond the re-read
scripts/backfill-snapshots.ts — delegates to projectsService.captureSnapshots(), not in scope of this shard
scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts — normalizeActionCodes: single findFirst + bulk UPDATE + recomputeChainFrom; no per-row SELECT loop
scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts — same pattern as normalize-action-codes; one-shot maintenance script
skills/dto/create-skill.dto.ts — single entity DTO, no arrays, no fan-out risk
skills/dto/update-skill.dto.ts — PartialType of CreateSkillDto, clean
skills/dto/assign-skill.dto.ts — two scalar fields (skillId + level), no arrays
skills/dto/update-skill-level.dto.ts — single level enum field, no arrays
departments/dto/create-department.dto.ts — scalar fields only with MaxLength, no arrays
departments/dto/update-department.dto.ts — PartialType of CreateDepartmentDto, clean
services/dto/create-service.dto.ts — scalar fields only with MaxLength and regex, no arrays
services/dto/update-service.dto.ts — PartialType of CreateServiceDto, clean
testing/testing.controller.ts — cleanDatabase is a truncate, not a loop; bounded operation
testing/testing.module.ts — module wiring only
audit/audit-action.compile-witness.ts — compile-time type witness only, no runtime code
audit/audit-payload-registry.compile-witness.ts — compile-time type witness only, no runtime code
audit/payload-schemas.ts — Zod schema definitions; parse is called per-insert (bounded), no loop
skills/skills.module.ts — module wiring only
departments/departments.module.ts — module wiring only
services/services.module.ts — module wiring only
personal-todos/personal-todos.module.ts — module wiring only
